---
title: "The Beautiful Frisch–Waugh–Lovell Theorem"

There are few results in econometrics that feel as magical as the [(Yule-)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.00369)[Frisch–Waugh–Lovell (FWL) Theorem](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.00369). In this article, we will trace how it is derived analytically and test if it holds empirically.

## What Does the FWL Theorem Says 🦊

For a multivariate [OLS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ordinary_least_squares) model

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= X_1\beta_1 + X_2\beta_2 + \varepsilon \\
&= X \beta + \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

where

- $Y$: $n \times 1$ outcome vector where $n$ is number of examples

- $X_1$: $n \times k_1$ treatment vector where $k_1$ is number of features

- $X_2$: $n \times k_2$ control vector where $k_2$ is number of features

- $\beta_i$: $k_i \times 1$ coefficient vector

- $\varepsilon$: $n \times 1$ error vector

- $X$: $n \times (k_1+k_2)$ total regressor vector

- $\beta$: $(k_1+k_2) \times 1$ total coefficient vector

The FWL theorem states that $\hat{\beta_1}$ estimated from the full regression is exactly the same coefficient obtained from the following procedure:

1. Regress $Y$ on control $X_2$ and keep the residuals $\tilde{Y}=M_2Y$ (outcome residualization/nuisance regression)
2. Regress $X_1$ on control $X_2$ and keep the residuals $\tilde{X_1}=M_1 X$ (treatment residualization/nuisance regression)
3. Regress $\tilde{Y}$ on $\tilde{X_1}$. $\beta_1$ derived from this is exactly the same as one from the full regression (final residualized regression) 

Let us pause for a second and think about how preposterous it is. Monsieurs George Udny Yule, Ragnar Frisch, Frederick V. Waugh, and Michael C. Lovell are basically saying:

> Hey, look. Those coefficients you thought you could only derive by regressing the outcome on the whole set of control and treatment variables? Turns out you can somehow get the *exact same* estimation from regressing the residuals of the outcome on those of the treatment variables.

When I first learned about this, it was nothing short of black magic.

## The Projection Matrix and The Annihilator Matrix

One last preparation before we dive into the proof is understanding the **projection matrix** and the badass-sounding **annihilator matrix**. When we regress $Y$ on $X_2$, we derive coefficient $\alpha_2$ as

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= X_2 \alpha_2 + \varepsilon_2 \\
E[X_2'(Y-X_2 \alpha_2)] &= 0 \text{ ; population moment condition} \\
X_2'(Y-X_2 \hat{\alpha_2}) &= 0 \text{ ; sample moment condition} \\
X_2'Y - X_2'X_2 \hat{\alpha_2}&= 0 \\
\hat{\alpha_2}&= (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2'Y  \\
\hat{Y_2} &= X_2 \hat{\alpha_2}  \\
&= X_2 (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2' Y \\
&= P_2 Y
\end{aligned}
$$

where 

- $\varepsilon_2$: $n \times 1$ error vector of the outcome nuisance regression

- $\hat{Y_2}$: $n \times 1$ estimated values based on the outcome nuisance regression

We can think of $P_2 = X_2 (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2'$ as a function that transforms actual outcome $Y$ into the estimated outcome $Y_2$. We call $P_2$ the projection matrix (symmetrical $n \times n$), refering to the fact that it *projects* $Y$ onto subspace spanned by $X_2$. Another neat thing about $P_2$ is that it is an [idempotent matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idempotent_matrix):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= X_2 (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2' \\
P_2 P_2 &= X_2 (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2' X_2 (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2' \\
&= P_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The annihilator matrix is simply $M_2 = I - P_2$ (also symmetrical $n \times n$ and idemptotent) and multiplying it to $Y$ gives residuals of the outcome nuisance regression due to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{Y_2} &=  P_2 Y \\
M_2 &= I - P_2 \\
M_2 Y & = (I - P_2) Y \\
&=Y - P_2 Y \\
&= Y - \hat{Y_2} 
\end{aligned}
$$

And by the way:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_2 X_2 & = (I - P_2) X_2 \\
&=X_2 - P_2 X_2 \\
&= X_2 - X_2 (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2' X_2\\
&= X_2 - X_2\\
&=0
\end{aligned}
$$

That is, $M_2$ is [orthogonal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orthogonality#Statistics,_econometrics,_and_economics) to $P_2$, and it *annihilates* all components in the subspace spanned by $X_2$.

## The Proof

Let us start the proof with the moment condition to estimate $\beta_1$ from the full regression.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X'(Y-X \beta)] &= 0 \text{ ; population moment condition} \\
X'(Y-X \hat{\beta}) &= 0 \text{ ; sample moment condition} \\
&=\begin{bmatrix} X_1' \\[2mm] X_2' \end{bmatrix} (Y - X_1\hat\beta_1 - X_2\hat\beta_2) = 0 \text{ ; partitioned to }X_1, X_2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The sample moment conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1'(Y - X_1 \hat{\beta_1} - X_2 \hat{\beta_2})&=0 \\
X_2'(Y - X_1 \hat{\beta_1} - X_2 \hat{\beta_2})&=0
\end{aligned}
$$

First, derive $\beta_2$ from $X_2$-moment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_2'(Y - X_2 \hat{\beta_2} - X_2 \hat{\beta_2})&=0 \\
&=X_2'Y - X_2' X_1 \hat{\beta_1} - X_2'X_2 \hat{\beta_2} \\
X_2'X_2 \hat{\beta_2}&=X_2'Y - X_2' X_1 \hat{\beta_1} \\
\hat{\beta_2} &= (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2' (Y-X_1 \hat{\beta_1})
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, substitute it into the derivation of $\beta_1$ from $X_1$-moment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1'(Y - X_1 \hat{\beta_1} - X_2 \hat{\beta_2})&=0 \\
(X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2' (Y-X_1 \hat{\beta_1})&=\hat{\beta_2}  \\
X_1'\Big((Y - X_1 \hat{\beta_1}) - X_2 (X_2'X_2)^{-1} X_2' (Y-X_1 \hat{\beta_1}) \Big)&=0 \\
X_1'\Big((1-P_2)(Y - X_1 \hat{\beta_1})\Big) &= 0 \\
X_1'M_2(Y - X_1 \hat{\beta_1}) &= 0 \\
X_1'M_2X_1\hat{\beta_1} &= X_1'M_2Y \\
\hat{\beta_1} &=(X_1'M_2X_1)^{-1}X_1'M_2Y \\
\end{aligned}
$$

And do you know what this $\hat{\beta_1}$ looks like?

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_2Y &= M_2X_1 \beta_{r} + \varepsilon_{r} \\
E[(M_2X_1)'(M_2Y-M_2X_1 \beta_{r})] &= 0 \text{ ; population moment condition} \\
(M_2X_1)'(M_2Y-M_2X_1 \hat{\beta_{r}}) &= 0 \text{ ; sample moment condition} \\
X_1'M_2^2 Y - X_1'M_2^2X_1 \hat{\beta_{r}} &=0  \text{ ; } M_2 = M_2'\\
X_1'M_2 Y - X_1'M_2X_1 \hat{\beta_{r}} &=0  \text{ ; } M_2 = M_2'\\
\hat{\beta_{r}}&= (X_1'M_2X_1)^{-1}X_1'M_2Y \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Exactly* the coefficient $\hat{\beta_{r}}$ when we regress residualized $Y$ on residualized $X_1$.

## Don't Believe the Symbol Manipulation? Let's Run The Code

For those of you who, like me, are skeptical of mathematical equation hocus pocus, let us test the theorem empirically. We will simulate a 500k-sample dataset with one Gaussian treatment variable `X_treatment`, 100 control variables (from 40 Guassian features, 30 power-law features, and a 31-class categorical feature; to make it more exciting), and a continuous outcome `y`. We estimate the coefficient of `X_treatment` using both full OLS and the FWL procedure. The FWL theorem posits that they are identical.

::: {#6e530a61 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="show"}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder
import time

np.random.seed(112)
n = 500_000

# Treatment
X_treatment = np.random.normal(0, 1, n)

# Gaussian controls
X_gauss = np.random.normal(0, 1, (n, 40))

# Power-law controls
X_power = np.random.pareto(2, (n, 30))

# Categorical controls
cats = np.random.choice([f'C{i}' for i in range(31)], size=(n, 1))
enc = OneHotEncoder(drop='first').fit(cats)
X_cat = enc.transform(cats).toarray()

# Combine into design matrix
X_controls = np.hstack([X_gauss, X_power, X_cat])
print(f'Shape of dataset: {X_controls.shape}')
# Outcome based on true coefficients
beta_treatment = 2.0
beta_controls = np.random.uniform(-1, 1, X_controls.shape[1])
y = beta_treatment * X_treatment + X_controls @ beta_controls + np.random.normal(0, 1, n)

# True beta
print("True β_treatment:", beta_treatment)

# --- Full OLS ---
start_full = time.time()
X_full = sm.add_constant(np.column_stack([X_treatment, X_controls]))
model_full = sm.OLS(y, X_full).fit()
coef_full = model_full.params[1]
end_full = time.time()
print("Full OLS β_treatment:", coef_full)
print(f"Full OLS computation time: {end_full - start_full:.3f} seconds")

# --- FWL ---
start_FWL = time.time()

# Step 1: residualize y on controls
X2 = sm.add_constant(X_controls)
y_resid = sm.OLS(y, X2).fit().resid

# Step 2: residualize treatment on controls
X_treatment_resid = sm.OLS(X_treatment, X2).fit().resid

# Step 3: regress residuals
coef_FWL = sm.OLS(y_resid, X_treatment_resid).fit().params[0]

end_FWL = time.time()

print("FWL β_treatment:", coef_FWL)
print(f"FWL computation time: {end_FWL - start_FWL:.3f} seconds")

# Compare
print("Are they numerically identical?", np.allclose(coef_full, coef_FWL))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Shape of dataset: (500000, 100)
True β_treatment: 2.0
Full OLS β_treatment: 2.0026663267823794
Full OLS computation time: 2.798 seconds
FWL β_treatment: 2.0026663267823746
FWL computation time: 4.497 seconds
Are they numerically identical? True
```
:::
:::


A noteworthy observation is that FWL implementation takes longer to compute even though theoretically it is doing matrix operations on a much smaller $k_1$ features. This points towards modern Python library optimizing the matrix operations so much that most of the computation lies in the overhead of repeating the (smaller) regression 3 times. I would need to dive deeper for confirmation. Better studied readers please let me know if you can pinpoint the reason why.
